pages.h1: How to deploy a Laravel app on Cloudways (2026)
pages.title: Deploy a Laravel App on Cloudways — Step by Step (2026)
pages.meta_description: A clear, copy-paste guide to deploying a Laravel 11 app on Cloudways: server setup, Git deploy, .env, migrations, queue workers, and SSL.
pages.intro: Cloudways is the path of least resistance for a Laravel app: the PHP version, Composer, and the web server are already tuned, so you skip the part where you fight Nginx at 2 a.m. This guide takes you from a fresh server to a live app with migrations, queues, and HTTPS — no prior server experience assumed.

blocks.text: 1. Launch a server and application

After signing up, create a server and pick the PHP stack. Choose a provider region close to your users and the smallest plan to start — you can scale later without rebuilding. Cloudways provisions an 'application' slot for you, which is where your Laravel code will live.
Once the server is up, note the public IP, the SSH/SFTP credentials, and the application's public path (it ends in /public_html — your Laravel public/ folder maps here).
2. Push your code with Git
From the application dashboard, open the Deployment via Git panel, add your repository SSH key, and pull the main branch. This is cleaner than SFTP because every future deploy is one click.

# locally, make sure your repo is clean
git add .
git commit -m "production build"
git push origin main

# then in Cloudways: Deployment via Git > Pull
3. Configure the environment
SSH into the server and set up your .env. Never commit secrets — generate the app key on the server.

cp .env.example .env
nano .env   # set APP_ENV=production, APP_DEBUG=false, DB_*, etc.
php artisan key:generate
composer install --optimize-autoloader --no-dev
4. Migrate and cache

php artisan migrate --force
php artisan config:cache
php artisan route:cache
php artisan view:cache
The --force flag is required in production because Laravel asks for confirmation otherwise. Caching config and routes gives a real speed bump on every request.
5. Queues and scheduled tasks
If your app sends mail or processes jobs, add a queue worker as a Supervisor process from the dashboard, and point the cron at the scheduler so anything in your Kernel runs on time.
* * * * * php /path/to/artisan schedule:run >> /dev/null 2>&1
6. Add a domain and SSL
Point your domain's A record at the server IP, add the domain in the dashboard, then enable the free Let's Encrypt certificate. Set APP_URL in .env to the https domain and re-run config:cache.

faq.q: Which PHP version should I pick for Laravel 11?
faq.a: Laravel 11 requires PHP 8.2 or higher. Pick 8.3 on Cloudways for the best balance of speed and library support in 2026.

faq.q: Do I need a separate database server?
faq.a: No. The MySQL (or MariaDB) instance ships on the same server. Create the database from the dashboard and copy the credentials into your .env.

faq.q: My CSS/JS isn't loading after deploy — why?
faq.a: You likely didn't build assets. Run 'npm install && npm run build' locally and commit the compiled files, or run the build step on the server.

pages.h1: How to deploy a Laravel app on a Hostinger VPS (2026)
pages.title: Deploy Laravel on a Hostinger VPS — Beginner Guide (2026)
pages.meta_description: Deploy a Laravel 11 app on a cheap Hostinger VPS: install PHP, Composer, Nginx, set up the site, run migrations, and add a free SSL certificate.
pages.intro: A VPS is the budget move when you want control and the lowest monthly bill — perfect on a student budget. The trade-off is you set up the server yourself. This guide does exactly that on a Hostinger VPS, command by command, with nothing assumed.

blocks.text: 1. Spin up the VPS
Create a VPS and choose the Ubuntu 24.04 image (or the pre-built Laravel/LEMP template if offered — it saves the next two steps). Set a root password and note the IP address.
2. Install the stack

ssh root@YOUR_SERVER_IP
apt update && apt upgrade -y
apt install -y nginx php8.3-fpm php8.3-mysql php8.3-mbstring \
  php8.3-xml php8.3-curl php8.3-zip unzip git

# Composer
curl -sS https://getcomposer.org/installer | php
mv composer.phar /usr/local/bin/composer
3. Pull the code

cd /var/www
git clone https://github.com/you/your-app.git
cd your-app
composer install --optimize-autoloader --no-dev
cp .env.example .env
php artisan key:generate
4. Point Nginx at public/
The single most common beginner mistake is serving the project root instead of the public/ folder. Your Nginx root must end in /public.
5. Permissions, migrate, SSL

chown -R www-data:www-data storage bootstrap/cache
php artisan migrate --force

# free SSL
apt install -y certbot python3-certbot-nginx
certbot --nginx -d yourdomain.com

faq.q: VPS or managed hosting for a first Laravel deploy?
faq.a: If you want to learn the server side, a VPS teaches you the most for the lowest cost. If you just want the app live with zero server admin, managed hosting is faster.

faq.q: I get '502 Bad Gateway' — what's wrong?
faq.a: Almost always the PHP-FPM socket path in your Nginx config doesn't match your PHP version. Check 'ls /run/php/' and update fastcgi_pass to the socket that exists.

pages.h1: How to fix a Laravel 500 error after deploying
pages.title: Fix the Laravel 500 Error After Deploy (Checklist)
pages.meta_description: A blank 500 page after deploying Laravel almost always comes down to five things. Work through this checklist to find and fix the cause in minutes.
pages.intro: A 500 error with a blank page after deploy is Laravel hiding the real exception because APP_DEBUG is off (correctly). The fix is to find the real error in the logs, then resolve one of a handful of common causes. Here's the checklist in the order that finds it fastest.

blocks.text: First: read the actual error
tail -n 50 storage/logs/laravel.log
Whatever the blank page hides is written here. If the log is empty or unwritable, that itself is the problem — jump to the permissions section.
Cause 1 — Missing or stale app key

php artisan key:generate
php artisan config:clear
Cause 2 — Storage / cache not writable

chmod -R 775 storage bootstrap/cache
chown -R www-data:www-data storage bootstrap/cache
Cause 3 — Stale cached config pointing at the old environment

php artisan config:clear
php artisan cache:clear
php artisan config:cache
Cause 4 — Composer dev dependencies missing
If you deployed with --no-dev but your code references a dev-only package, you'll 500. Move the package out of require-dev or reinstall.
Cause 5 — Wrong document root
If the web server points at the project root instead of public/, framework files leak and routing breaks. Confirm the root ends in /public.

faq.q: Should I just set APP_DEBUG=true to see the error?
faq.a: Temporarily, on a server only you can reach, yes — it shows the stack trace. Turn it back off immediately; leaving debug on in production leaks secrets and file paths.

faq.q: It works locally but 500s in production. Why?
faq.a: The difference is almost always environment: a missing .env value, a cached config from another environment, or file permissions. The log will name the specific one.

pages.h1: Livewire vs Inertia for Laravel: which to choose in 2026
pages.title: Livewire vs Inertia (2026): Picking a Laravel Frontend
pages.meta_description: A practical comparison of Livewire and Inertia.js for Laravel apps in 2026 — how each works, when each wins, and how to decide for your project.
pages.intro: Both keep you inside Laravel instead of building a separate API and SPA, but they make opposite bets. Livewire renders on the server and ships HTML over the wire; Inertia ships a real JavaScript frontend (Vue or React) that talks to Laravel as the backend. The right pick depends on how much frontend interactivity you actually need.

blocks.text: How Livewire works

You write components in PHP and Blade. Interactions make a round-trip to the server, which re-renders the component and swaps the changed HTML. You barely write JavaScript. This is the fastest way to ship for a PHP-first developer.
How Inertia works

You write Vue or React pages, and Inertia handles the routing glue so there's no separate API layer. You get a genuinely rich client-side app while still using Laravel controllers and Eloquent on the backend.
When Livewire wins

items: You're comfortable in PHP and want minimal JavaScript
Dashboards, CRUD, and forms — interactivity is moderate
You want the smallest possible learning curve and toolchain

blocks.text: When Inertia wins

items: You already know Vue or React well
The UI is genuinely app-like: drag-and-drop, heavy client state, offline bits
You want the frontend to be portable to other backends later

blocks.text: The honest default

If you're a backend-leaning Laravel developer shipping a typical SaaS or internal tool, start with Livewire — you'll ship sooner. Reach for Inertia when the interactivity clearly outgrows server round-trips.

faq.q: Can I use both in one app?
faq.a: Yes, but it's rarely worth the complexity. Pick one as the default and only mix if a single screen has very different needs.

faq.q: Which is faster?
faq.a: For perceived speed on rich UIs, Inertia (client-side) often feels snappier. For simple pages and time-to-ship, Livewire wins. Hosting and query optimization matter more than the choice for most apps.

pages.h1: How to set up queues in Laravel (with a worker that stays alive)
pages.title: Set Up Laravel Queues + a Persistent Worker (2026)
pages.meta_description: Configure Laravel queues end to end: choose a driver, dispatch jobs, run a worker with Supervisor so it survives reboots, and monitor failures.
pages.intro: Queues move slow work — sending mail, processing uploads, calling external APIs — out of the request so your app stays fast. The part that trips people up isn't dispatching a job; it's keeping the worker running in production. Here's both.

blocks.text: 1. Pick a driver
Set QUEUE_CONNECTION in .env. 'database' is the zero-extra-infrastructure choice and is fine for most apps; 'redis' is faster and scales further if you already run Redis.

# for the database driver
php artisan make:queue-table
php artisan migrate
2. Dispatch a job
php artisan make:job ProcessUpload

use App\Jobs\ProcessUpload;

ProcessUpload::dispatch($upload);
3. Keep the worker alive with Supervisor
Running 'php artisan queue:work' in a terminal dies when the terminal closes. Supervisor restarts it automatically and on reboot.

[program:laravel-worker]
command=php /var/www/your-app/artisan queue:work --sleep=3 --tries=3
autostart=true
autorestart=true
user=www-data
numprocs=1
redirect_stderr=true
stdout_logfile=/var/www/your-app/storage/logs/worker.log
4. Always restart workers after deploy
Workers hold your code in memory, so old code keeps running until you restart them. Add this to your deploy script:
php artisan queue:restart

faq.q: Why does my job run old code after I deploy?
faq.a: The worker booted with the previous version in memory. Run 'php artisan queue:restart' at the end of every deploy.

faq.q: How do I see failed jobs?
faq.a: Failed jobs land in the failed_jobs table. View them with 'php artisan queue:failed' and retry with 'php artisan queue:retry all'.

pages.h1: Fix Laravel 'failed to open stream: Permission denied' on storage
pages.title: Fix Laravel Storage Permission Denied Error
pages.meta_description: The storage permission error after deploy comes from the web server not owning storage/ and bootstrap/cache/. Here's the correct, safe fix.
pages.intro: This error means the user running PHP (usually www-data) can't write to storage/ or bootstrap/cache/. The fix is to give ownership to the web server user — not to chmod 777, which is the common but insecure 'fix' you'll see in forum answers.

blocks.text: The correct fix

sudo chown -R www-data:www-data storage bootstrap/cache
sudo chmod -R 775 storage bootstrap/cache
Why not 777?
777 lets any user on the server write to your app's storage, which is a real security risk. 775 with correct ownership gives the web server write access and keeps everyone else out.
If it comes back after deploy
A Git pull can reset ownership on new files. Add the chown line to the end of your deploy script so permissions are always correct after a release.

faq.q: How do I know which user runs PHP?
faq.a: Run 'ps aux | grep php-fpm'. The user column on the worker processes is the one that needs to own storage — commonly www-data on Ubuntu.

pages.h1: How to deploy a Laravel app on a DigitalOcean droplet (2026)
pages.title: Deploy Laravel on a DigitalOcean Droplet — Full Guide (2026)
pages.meta_description: Deploy a Laravel 11 app on a DigitalOcean droplet from scratch: create the droplet, install the LEMP stack, deploy your code, run migrations, and add free SSL.
pages.intro: A DigitalOcean droplet gives you a clean Ubuntu box and full control for a few dollars a month — and new accounts start with credit (around $200) that covers months of learning for free. The cost is that you build the stack yourself. This guide does it end to end on a fresh droplet, with every command spelled out.

blocks.text: 1. Create the droplet

Create a droplet with the Ubuntu 24.04 LTS image and the cheapest shared-CPU plan to start. Add your SSH key during creation so you can log in without a password, pick a region near your users, and note the public IP once it boots.
2. Install the LEMP stack
Log in as root and install Nginx, PHP 8.3 with the extensions Laravel needs, and MySQL. Composer comes next.

ssh root@YOUR_DROPLET_IP
apt update && apt upgrade -y
apt install -y nginx mysql-server git unzip \
  php8.3-fpm php8.3-mysql php8.3-mbstring php8.3-xml \
  php8.3-curl php8.3-zip php8.3-bcmath

curl -sS https://getcomposer.org/installer | php
mv composer.phar /usr/local/bin/composer
3. Create the database

mysql -e "CREATE DATABASE app;"
mysql -e "CREATE USER 'app'@'localhost' IDENTIFIED BY 'a-strong-password';"
mysql -e "GRANT ALL ON app.* TO 'app'@'localhost'; FLUSH PRIVILEGES;"
4. Pull and configure the app

cd /var/www
git clone https://github.com/you/your-app.git
cd your-app
composer install --optimize-autoloader --no-dev
cp .env.example .env
php artisan key:generate
# edit .env: APP_ENV=production, APP_DEBUG=false,
# DB_DATABASE=app, DB_USERNAME=app, DB_PASSWORD=...
php artisan migrate --force
5. Point Nginx at public/
The classic mistake is serving the project root. The Nginx root must end in /public, and PHP requests must be handed to the php8.3-fpm socket.

chown -R www-data:www-data storage bootstrap/cache
ln -s /etc/nginx/sites-available/your-app /etc/nginx/sites-enabled/
nginx -t && systemctl reload nginx
6. Add a domain and free SSL
Point your domain's A record at the droplet IP, then let Certbot issue and auto-renew a free certificate.

apt install -y certbot python3-certbot-nginx
certbot --nginx -d yourdomain.com

faq.q: Should I use a droplet or App Platform?
faq.a: A droplet teaches you the server and is cheapest, especially with the starting credit. App Platform is managed and faster to set up but costs more and gives less control. For learning Laravel deployment, start with a droplet.

faq.q: Do I need Laravel Forge for this?
faq.a: No — the steps above are the whole job. Forge just automates them and manages the server for you, which is worth it once you run several sites and don't want to repeat this by hand.

faq.q: How do I deploy updates after the first time?
faq.a: SSH in, 'git pull', run 'composer install --no-dev', 'php artisan migrate --force', then cache config and routes. Automate it with a deploy script or GitHub Actions.

pages.h1: The best Laravel hosting in 2026 (honest comparison)
pages.title: Best Laravel Hosting in 2026 — Managed vs VPS Compared
pages.meta_description: A practical, no-hype comparison of the best ways to host a Laravel app in 2026 — managed (Cloudways), cheap VPS (Hostinger), and raw droplets (DigitalOcean) — with a clear pick for each situation.
pages.intro: There's no single 'best' Laravel host — there's the best one for how much server work you want to do. The real choice is between managed hosting (you never touch the server), a cheap VPS (you set it up once), and raw droplets (you control everything). Here's how the three main options actually compare, and which to pick.

blocks.text: What Laravel actually needs from a host

items: PHP 8.2+ with the common extensions (mbstring, xml, curl, pdo)
Shell access to run Composer and 'php artisan' commands — not plain shared hosting
A database (MySQL/MariaDB or PostgreSQL)
The ability to run a queue worker and the scheduler (cron)
The web root pointed at public/, plus free SSL

blocks.text: That list is why cheap shared hosting without SSH is a bad fit: you can't run artisan, workers, or the scheduler properly. Every option below clears that bar.
Option 1 — Managed cloud (Cloudways): least server work

Cloudways runs on top of providers like DigitalOcean and AWS but handles the server for you: PHP is pre-tuned, SSL is one click, and staging and Git deploy are built in — no Nginx config to write. You pay more than a raw VPS but spend zero time on server admin, the right trade when your time is worth more than a few dollars a month.
Pick it if: you want the app live today, you don't want to learn server administration yet, or you do client work and need staging and easy backups.
Option 2 — Cheap VPS (Hostinger): lowest bill, set up once

A Hostinger VPS is the budget-control option: you install the LEMP stack yourself once, then it's yours for a low monthly price. Some plans ship a Laravel/LEMP template that skips most of the setup. More work than managed, far cheaper over time.
Pick it if: you're on a tight budget, you want to learn the server side, and you're comfortable running a handful of setup commands.
Option 3 — Raw droplet (DigitalOcean): most control, credit to start

A DigitalOcean droplet is a clean Ubuntu box with nothing assumed. You get total control, and the new-account credit (around $200) covers months of a small droplet for free — ideal for learning. Pair it with Laravel Forge later if you want managed convenience without leaving the provider.
Pick it if: you want to understand every layer, you're experimenting on the free credit, or you plan to manage the server with Forge.
The honest recommendation

items: Want it live with no server work → Cloudways (managed).
Tight budget, willing to set it up once → Hostinger VPS.
Learning the stack or using free credit → DigitalOcean droplet.

blocks.text: If you're not sure, start managed: the time you save on server admin in the first month is worth more than the price difference, and you can always move to a raw VPS once you know exactly what you need.

faq.q: Can I host Laravel on cheap shared hosting?
faq.a: Sometimes, but it's painful: most shared plans lack SSH, Composer, and a way to run queue workers and the scheduler. Use a managed cloud or a VPS instead — both are affordable and built for apps like Laravel.

faq.q: Is managed hosting worth the extra cost?
faq.a: If your time has any value, usually yes early on. You skip server setup, security patching, and SSL config. Move to a raw VPS later when you want the lower bill and know the stack.

faq.q: Which is fastest?
faq.a: Raw performance is similar across all three on comparable hardware — they all run the same PHP. Your database queries, caching, and whether you ran 'config:cache' and 'route:cache' matter far more than the host's brand.

pages.h1: How to fix the '419 Page Expired' error in Laravel
pages.title: Fix Laravel '419 Page Expired' — Causes and Solutions
pages.meta_description: The Laravel 419 Page Expired error is a CSRF/session mismatch. Here are the real causes — missing token, expired session, wrong cookie domain, AJAX header, unwritable storage — and how to fix each.
pages.intro: A '419 Page Expired' response means Laravel rejected the request's CSRF token — either it was missing, or the session that would validate it couldn't be found. It almost always shows up on form submits and logins. Here are the causes in the order that finds it fastest.

blocks.text: What 419 actually means

419 is Laravel's own status for a failed CSRF check (handled by the VerifyCsrfToken middleware). The token sent with the request didn't match the one tied to the user's session. So the fix is always one of two things: the token wasn't sent, or the session wasn't readable.
Cause 1 — Missing @csrf in the form
Every non-GET HTML form needs the token. If you wrote a form by hand without it, you'll get a 419 on submit.

<form method="POST" action="/save">
    @csrf
    <!-- fields -->
</form>
Cause 2 — Stale token on a page left open

If a login page sat open past the session lifetime, its token expired — that is the literal 'page expired'. Reloading issues a fresh token. To make it less annoying for users, raise SESSION_LIFETIME in .env.
Cause 3 — Session cookie can't be set (domain / HTTPS mismatch)

If the session cookie isn't stored, every request looks token-less. After a deploy this is usually a config mismatch: SESSION_DOMAIN doesn't match your real domain, or SESSION_SECURE_COOKIE is true while you're still testing over plain HTTP.

# .env — match your real domain, only force secure cookies on HTTPS
SESSION_DOMAIN=yourdomain.com
SESSION_SECURE_COOKIE=true   # set false if you're not on HTTPS yet
APP_URL=https://yourdomain.com
Cause 4 — AJAX/SPA requests without the token header
JavaScript requests must send the token in the X-CSRF-TOKEN header. Expose it in a meta tag and attach it to your HTTP client.
<meta name="csrf-token" content="{{ csrf_token() }}">

axios.defaults.headers.common['X-CSRF-TOKEN'] =
  document.querySelector('meta[name="csrf-token"]').content;
Cause 5 — Session storage not writable

With the 'file' session driver, an unwritable storage/framework/sessions directory means no session is ever saved, so the token can never validate. Fix permissions, or move to a shared driver (database/redis) if you run more than one server.

chmod -R 775 storage bootstrap/cache
chown -R www-data:www-data storage bootstrap/cache
php artisan config:clear

faq.q: Why does 419 only happen in production, not locally?
faq.a: Locally you're usually on http://localhost with relaxed cookie settings. In production a wrong SESSION_DOMAIN, a secure-cookie flag over HTTP, or unwritable session storage breaks the cookie — so the token can't be validated.

faq.q: Does raising SESSION_LIFETIME fix it for good?
faq.a: Only the 'left the page open too long' case. If the cause is a missing token, the cookie domain, or permissions, a longer lifetime changes nothing — fix the actual cause above.

pages.h1: How to deploy Laravel automatically with GitHub Actions
pages.title: Deploy Laravel with GitHub Actions — CI/CD over SSH (2026)
pages.meta_description: Set up a GitHub Actions workflow that tests and deploys your Laravel app to a VPS over SSH on every push: run tests, deploy, migrate, and restart the queue worker.
pages.intro: Manually SSHing in to 'git pull' on every change gets old fast and is easy to get wrong. A GitHub Actions workflow runs your tests and then deploys to your server automatically on every push to main — the same steps you'd type by hand, but reliable and logged. Here's a complete setup for a VPS.

blocks.text: 1. Add deploy secrets to the repo
In your GitHub repo, open Settings → Secrets and variables → Actions and add the server host, user, and a private SSH key that's authorized on the server. Never put these in the workflow file itself.

items: SSH_HOST — your server IP or domain
SSH_USER — the deploy user (e.g. a dedicated 'deployer')
SSH_KEY — the private key whose public half is in the server's authorized_keys

blocks.text: 2. Create the workflow file
Add this as .github/workflows/deploy.yml. It runs the test suite first and only deploys if tests pass.

name: Deploy

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: shivammathur/setup-php@v2
        with:
          php-version: '8.3'
      - run: composer install --no-interaction --prefer-dist
      - run: cp .env.example .env && php artisan key:generate
      - run: php artisan test

  deploy:
    needs: test
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Deploy over SSH
        uses: appleboy/ssh-action@v1.0.3
        with:
          host: ${{ secrets.SSH_HOST }}
          username: ${{ secrets.SSH_USER }}
          key: ${{ secrets.SSH_KEY }}
          script: |
            cd /var/www/your-app
            git pull origin main
            composer install --optimize-autoloader --no-dev
            php artisan migrate --force
            php artisan config:cache
            php artisan route:cache
            php artisan queue:restart
3. Why each deploy step matters

items: 'migrate --force' applies schema changes without an interactive prompt.
'config:cache' and 'route:cache' rebuild caches so they don't serve old values.
'queue:restart' is critical — workers hold old code in memory until you restart them.

blocks.text: 4. Push and watch it run
Commit the workflow, push to main, and open the Actions tab. You'll see the test job run, then the deploy job. From now on, shipping is just 'git push' — exactly the low-effort loop you want.

git add .github/workflows/deploy.yml
git commit -m "add CI/CD deploy"
git push origin main

faq.q: Should deploy run if tests fail?
faq.a: No. The 'needs: test' line makes the deploy job wait for tests to pass, so a broken commit never reaches the server. That safety is the main reason to use CI/CD over a manual pull.

faq.q: How do I avoid downtime during deploy?
faq.a: For true zero-downtime, deploy into a new release folder and switch a 'current' symlink (the approach Deployer and Laravel Envoyer use). The pull-in-place workflow here is simpler and fine for small apps; add atomic releases when traffic grows.

faq.q: What user should run the deploy?
faq.a: A dedicated deploy user with write access to the app directory is cleanest. Keep file ownership correct so the web server can read the code and write to storage/.

pages.h1: How to fix 'Vite manifest not found' in Laravel
pages.title: Fix 'Vite manifest not found' in Laravel — Causes & Fix
pages.meta_description: The 'Vite manifest not found' error means Laravel can't find public/build/manifest.json. Here's why it happens after deploy and how to fix it for good.
keywords: unable to locate file in vite manifest
pages.intro: This error means the @vite directive looked for public/build/manifest.json and it wasn't there. That file is created only when you compile your assets with Vite — so the fix is almost always 'build the assets and make sure the compiled files actually reach the server.' Here's the full picture.

blocks.text: What the manifest is

Laravel's @vite() Blade directive reads public/build/manifest.json to know which compiled CSS/JS to load. That file exists only after a production build; in local development the Vite dev server serves assets instead, which is why you never see this error there.
Cause 1 — You never built the assets
On the server (or in CI) run the production build — you need Node installed. This creates public/build/, including manifest.json.

npm install
npm run build
Cause 2 — public/build was never deployed

If you build locally but public/build is git-ignored (the Laravel default ignores it), the compiled files never reach production. Either build on the server, or build in CI and ship public/build with the deploy.
Cause 3 — A stale 'hot' file from dev mode
If a leftover public/hot file exists, Laravel tries to load assets from the Vite dev server — which isn't running in production. Delete it.
rm -f public/hot
The reliable fix — build during deploy
The cleanest setup builds assets on every deploy so production always has a fresh manifest. Add it to your deploy script:

npm ci
npm run build
php artisan view:cache

faq.q: Why does it work locally but break in production?
faq.a: Locally you run 'npm run dev', which starts the Vite dev server and writes public/hot — no manifest needed. Production has no dev server, so it requires the compiled public/build/manifest.json from 'npm run build'.

faq.q: Do I commit public/build or build on the server?
faq.a: Either works. Building on the server or in CI keeps the repo clean and is the common choice — just make sure Node is available there. Committing the built files is fine too if your host has no Node.

pages.h1: How to fix 'could not find driver' (PDO) in Laravel
pages.title: Fix Laravel 'could not find driver' — PDO Extension Fix
pages.meta_description: Laravel's 'could not find driver' is a PDOException: the database driver extension isn't loaded. Here's how to install and enable the right PDO driver and verify it.
keywords: laravel pdo could not find driver
pages.intro: The 'could not find driver' error is a PDOException: PHP doesn't have the database driver extension loaded for the database you configured. It's most common right after setting up a new server. The fix is to install and enable the right PDO extension, then restart PHP.

blocks.text: What's missing

Laravel talks to your database through PHP's PDO extension. If DB_CONNECTION=mysql but pdo_mysql isn't installed (or you use pgsql/sqlite without their drivers), PHP has no driver to use and throws 'could not find driver'.
Step 1 — Confirm which driver you need

grep DB_CONNECTION .env
# mysql -> pdo_mysql, pgsql -> pdo_pgsql, sqlite -> pdo_sqlite
Step 2 — Install the extension (Ubuntu/Debian)

# MySQL / MariaDB
sudo apt install -y php8.3-mysql

# PostgreSQL
sudo apt install -y php8.3-pgsql

# then restart PHP and the web server
sudo systemctl restart php8.3-fpm nginx
Step 3 — Verify it's loaded
php -m | grep pdo
You should see pdo_mysql (or your driver) in the list. If your app runs under PHP-FPM, install the extension for the same PHP version FPM uses — installing it for the CLI PHP only is a common trap.
If you use SQLite

sudo apt install -y php8.3-sqlite3
touch database/database.sqlite

faq.q: It works on the CLI but not in the browser (or vice-versa)
faq.a: CLI PHP and PHP-FPM can be different versions with different extensions. Run 'php -v', check your FPM version, and install the extension for the version your web server actually uses.

faq.q: I'm on managed hosting — how do I enable it?
faq.a: Managed hosts like Cloudways ship the common PDO drivers already enabled, which is why you rarely hit this there. On a raw VPS you install them yourself, as above.

pages.h1: How to fix 'Target class controller does not exist' in Laravel
pages.title: Fix 'Target class controller does not exist' in Laravel
pages.meta_description: Laravel's 'Target class [Controller] does not exist' is usually the post-Laravel-8 route namespace change, a wrong namespace, or a stale cache. Here's how to fix each.
keywords: target class controller does not exist
pages.intro: 'Target class [X] does not exist' means the router or container tried to resolve a class it couldn't find. In routes it's almost always the post-Laravel-8 namespace change; elsewhere it's a typo, a missing import, or a stale cache. Here's how to pin it down.

blocks.text: Most common — string controller without the full namespace
Since Laravel 8 there's no automatic App\Http\Controllers prefix on route strings. Use the class-based (callable array) syntax — it's the recommended form and your IDE can check it.

use App\Http\Controllers\PostController;

Route::get('/posts', [PostController::class, 'index']);

If you insist on string syntax, give the full namespace: 'App\Http\Controllers\PostController@index' — or re-add the namespace prefix in your RouteServiceProvider. The class-array form above is cleaner.
Cause 2 — Wrong namespace or filename

A class's namespace must match its folder, and the filename must match the class name exactly (case-sensitive on Linux, even if it worked on Windows/macOS). A controller in app/Http/Controllers/Admin must be namespaced App\Http\Controllers\Admin.
Cause 3 — Composer hasn't picked up the class
composer dump-autoload
Cause 4 — Stale route/config cache
If you cached routes before adding or renaming the controller, the cache still points at the old map.
php artisan optimize:clear

faq.q: It works locally but 500s on the server
faq.a: Linux file systems are case-sensitive. 'postcontroller.php' resolves on macOS/Windows but not on a Linux server. Make the filename and class name match exactly, then run 'composer dump-autoload'.

pages.h1: How to fix 'SQLSTATE[HY000] [1045] Access denied' in Laravel
pages.title: Fix Laravel SQLSTATE 1045 Access Denied (Database)
pages.meta_description: SQLSTATE 1045 Access denied is MySQL rejecting Laravel's database credentials. Here's the checklist: wrong .env values, missing user/grants, wrong host, cached config.
pages.intro: SQLSTATE[HY000] [1045] Access denied is MySQL rejecting the username/password Laravel sent. It's a credentials or privileges problem, not a Laravel bug. After a deploy it's nearly always a .env mismatch or a cached config. Here's the checklist.

blocks.text: Step 1 — Check the credentials in .env
grep '^DB_' .env
Confirm DB_DATABASE, DB_USERNAME, and DB_PASSWORD exactly match a MySQL user that exists. A trailing space or stray quote in the password is a classic cause.
Step 2 — Test the login directly
Bypass Laravel and try the exact credentials against MySQL. If this fails, the problem is MySQL, not your app.
mysql -u your_db_user -p your_db_name
Step 3 — Create/fix the user and grant access

mysql -e "CREATE USER IF NOT EXISTS 'app'@'localhost' IDENTIFIED BY 'strong-pass';"
mysql -e "GRANT ALL ON your_db_name.* TO 'app'@'localhost'; FLUSH PRIVILEGES;"
Step 4 — Clear the cached config
Laravel caches .env into config. If you fixed .env but the old values were cached, you'll still get 1045.

php artisan config:clear
php artisan config:cache
Common gotcha — wrong host
If your database is on another server, 'localhost' won't work and the user must be allowed from your app server's host (e.g. 'app'@'%' or a specific IP). Set DB_HOST to the real database host.

faq.q: Same credentials work locally but not in production
faq.a: Production MySQL has its own users and grants. The user on your laptop doesn't exist on the server. Create the user and grant it access on the production database, then clear the cached config.

faq.q: I changed .env but nothing happened
faq.a: You almost certainly have a cached config. Run 'php artisan config:clear' (and re-cache with 'config:cache' in production). Laravel reads cached config before .env when a cache exists.

pages.h1: How to schedule a recurring task in Laravel
pages.title: How to Schedule Recurring Tasks in Laravel (2026 Guide)
pages.meta_description: Set up Laravel's scheduler end to end: define tasks in PHP, add the single cron entry the docs assume you know, test without waiting, and avoid overlapping runs.
pages.intro: Laravel's scheduler lets you define recurring jobs in PHP instead of editing crontab for each one. You add a single cron entry once, and Laravel runs everything you've scheduled at the right time. Here's the full setup — including the one server step people forget.

blocks.text: 1. Define the schedule (Laravel 11)
In Laravel 11 you schedule tasks in routes/console.php with the Schedule facade. Older apps use the schedule() method in app/Console/Kernel.php — the API is the same.
2. Or schedule a queued job

use App\Jobs\PruneOldRecords;

Schedule::job(new PruneOldRecords)->weekly();
3. Add the ONE cron entry on the server
This is the step people miss. The scheduler only runs if cron calls schedule:run every minute. Add this single line to your crontab — you never touch cron again after this.
* * * * * cd /var/www/your-app && php artisan schedule:run >> /dev/null 2>&1
4. Test it without waiting

php artisan schedule:list   # see everything that's scheduled
php artisan schedule:run    # run anything due right now
5. Avoid overlaps and set a timezone
For long tasks, stop a second copy starting before the first finishes. You can also pin a timezone per task.

Schedule::command('reports:build')
    ->dailyAt('02:00')
    ->timezone('Africa/Casablanca')
    ->withoutOverlapping();

faq.q: My scheduled tasks never run on the server
faq.a: You almost certainly didn't add the cron entry that calls 'php artisan schedule:run' every minute. Without it the scheduler is never triggered. Add the single crontab line shown above.

faq.q: Do I need Supervisor for the scheduler?
faq.a: No — Supervisor is for queue workers (long-running). The scheduler just needs the one-minute cron entry. Managed hosts like Cloudways let you add this cron from the dashboard.

pages.h1: How to build a REST API with Sanctum in Laravel
pages.title: Build a REST API with Laravel Sanctum (Token Auth, 2026)
pages.meta_description: A complete, minimal Laravel REST API with Sanctum token auth: install the API, issue tokens on login, protect routes, call them with a Bearer token, and log out.
pages.intro: Sanctum is the simplest way to add token authentication to a Laravel API — ideal for a mobile app or SPA backend. In Laravel 11 it's one install command away. Here's a complete, minimal REST API with login, protected routes, and token issuing.

blocks.text: 1. Install the API + Sanctum
Laravel 11 ships API scaffolding behind one command. It installs Sanctum, publishes its migration, and creates routes/api.php.

php artisan install:api
php artisan migrate
2. Let the User model issue tokens
3. A login route that returns a token
4. Protect routes with the sanctum guard
5. Call the API with the token
Clients send the token as a Bearer header on every protected request.

curl https://yourapi.com/api/me \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_TOKEN" \
  -H "Accept: application/json"
6. Log out (revoke the token)

faq.q: Sanctum tokens or Passport (OAuth)?
faq.a: For first-party apps — your own mobile app or SPA — Sanctum is simpler and enough. Reach for Passport only when you need full OAuth2 (third-party clients acting on a user's behalf).

faq.q: SPA on the same domain — tokens or cookies?
faq.a: For a SPA on the same top-level domain, Sanctum's cookie-based session auth is more secure than storing tokens in JavaScript. Use the Bearer-token flow for mobile apps and cross-domain clients.

pages.h1: How to fix '1452 a foreign key constraint fails' in Laravel
pages.title: Fix Laravel 1452 Foreign Key Constraint Fails
pages.meta_description: Error 1452 means you saved a child row whose foreign key points at a parent that doesn't exist. Here are the real causes — insert order, type mismatch, bad id — and the fix.
keywords: cannot add or update a child row
pages.intro: Error 1452 — 'Cannot add or update a child row: a foreign key constraint fails' — means you tried to save a row whose foreign key points at a parent row that doesn't exist (or is the wrong type). It's a data/order problem, not a Laravel bug. Here's how to find and fix it.

blocks.text: What triggers 1452

MySQL refuses a child row whose foreign key value has no matching row in the parent table. Common causes: inserting the child before the parent, a null/zero foreign key, seeders running in the wrong order, or a type mismatch between the two columns.
Cause 1 — Wrong insert/seed order
Create parents before children. In seeders and factories, seed users before posts, categories before products, and so on.

User::factory(10)->create();
Post::factory(50)->create(); // each picks an existing user_id
Cause 2 — Foreign key column type mismatch
The foreign key and the referenced primary key must be the same type. If id is BIGINT UNSIGNED (the Laravel default), the foreign key must be too — foreignId() handles this for you.
$table->foreignId('user_id')->constrained()->cascadeOnDelete();
Find the orphaned rows
SELECT user_id FROM posts WHERE user_id NOT IN (SELECT id FROM users);

faq.q: It happens only during migrate:fresh --seed
faq.a: Your seeders run in the wrong order. Seed parent tables first, or use factory relationships (->has() / ->for()) so the parent is created automatically.

pages.h1: How to fix 'Specified key was too long' (error 1071) in Laravel
pages.title: Fix Laravel 'Specified Key Was Too Long' (1071)
pages.meta_description: Error 1071 on migrate is the utf8mb4 index-length limit on older MySQL/MariaDB. The classic Laravel fix is one line: Schema::defaultStringLength(191). Here's why and how.
keywords: specified key was too long laravel
pages.intro: Error 1071 — 'Specified key was too long; max key length is 767 bytes' — appears on migrate with older MySQL/MariaDB using utf8mb4. The default string index is longer than the engine allows. The classic Laravel fix is one line in a service provider.

blocks.text: Why it happens

utf8mb4 uses up to 4 bytes per character, so a VARCHAR(255) index needs 1020 bytes — over the 767-byte limit on MySQL < 5.7.7 and older MariaDB. Newer versions raise the limit, so upgrading the database also fixes it.
The standard fix — set a default string length
Set a shorter default index length in AppServiceProvider::boot(). 191 is the safe value for utf8mb4 under the old limit.
Then re-run migrations
php artisan migrate:fresh
Better long-term — upgrade the database
MySQL 5.7.7+ and MariaDB 10.2+ don't have this limit. If you control the server, upgrading is the cleaner fix; managed hosts usually run a recent version already.

faq.q: Is capping string length at 191 safe?
faq.a: Yes for almost all apps — it only limits the length of indexed string columns. You rarely need an index on a string longer than 191 characters; for long text, use a TEXT column with a fulltext index instead.

pages.h1: How to fix 'Route [name] not defined' in Laravel
pages.title: Fix Laravel 'Route Not Defined' — Causes and Fix
pages.meta_description: 'Route [name] not defined' means you referenced a named route Laravel can't find. Usually a missing ->name(), a typo, or a stale route cache. Here's the fast checklist.
pages.intro: 'Route [name] not defined' means you referenced a named route (via route() or a redirect) that Laravel can't find. It's almost always a missing name, a typo, or a stale route cache. Here's the fast checklist.

blocks.text: Cause 1 — The route has no name
route('dashboard') only works if a route is named 'dashboard'. Add ->name() to the route definition.

Route::get('/dashboard', [DashboardController::class, 'index'])
    ->name('dashboard');
Cause 2 — Typo or wrong name
Names are exact strings: 'users.edit' and 'user.edit' are different. List the route names to see what actually exists.
php artisan route:list --name=dashboard
Cause 3 — Stale route cache
If you cached routes and then added or renamed one, the cache doesn't know about it.

php artisan route:clear
# in production, re-cache afterwards:
php artisan route:cache
Cause 4 — Route not actually loaded
A named route in a file that isn't loaded won't resolve. Confirm it appears in 'php artisan route:list'.

faq.q: It works locally but not after deploy
faq.a: You almost certainly have a cached route table from before the route existed. Run 'php artisan route:clear' then 'php artisan route:cache' as part of your deploy.

pages.h1: How to fix 'Maximum execution time exceeded' in Laravel
pages.title: Fix Laravel 'Maximum Execution Time Exceeded'
pages.meta_description: The 'maximum execution time exceeded' error means a request ran too long and PHP killed it. The real fix is usually to queue the slow work, not just raise the limit. Here's both.
pages.intro: 'Maximum execution time of N seconds exceeded' means a request ran longer than PHP's time limit and was killed. The right fix is usually to move the slow work off the request — not just raise the limit. Here's both.

blocks.text: First: should this run in a request at all?
If the slow work is sending mail, processing a file, or calling an external API, push it to a queue so the request returns instantly. Raising the time limit only hides the problem.
ProcessReport::dispatch($report); // returns immediately
If you genuinely need longer (CLI / long jobs)
For legitimate long-running CLI tasks, raise PHP's max_execution_time. Keep web requests short.

; php.ini
max_execution_time = 120
On a VPS, also raise the web server timeout
Nginx + PHP-FPM has its own timeout, so raising only PHP isn't enough. Increase fastcgi_read_timeout (and the FPM request_terminate_timeout) to match.

faq.q: Why not just set the limit very high?
faq.a: A long time limit ties up a PHP worker, so a few slow requests can exhaust your workers and take the whole site down. Queue the work instead and keep request limits short.

pages.h1: How to send email with a Mailable in Laravel
pages.title: Send Email with a Mailable in Laravel (2026 Guide)
pages.meta_description: Send email in Laravel the clean way: configure a mailer, generate a Mailable, pass data to a Markdown view, and queue it so the request stays fast.
pages.intro: Laravel's Mailable classes give you a clean, testable way to build and send email. Here's the full path: configure a mailer, generate a Mailable, write the view, and send it — synchronously or, better, on a queue.

blocks.text: 1. Configure a mailer
Set your provider in .env. For real delivery use an SMTP/API service; for local testing, the 'log' mailer writes emails to storage/logs.

MAIL_MAILER=smtp
MAIL_HOST=smtp.yourprovider.com
MAIL_PORT=587
MAIL_USERNAME=...
MAIL_PASSWORD=...
MAIL_FROM_ADDRESS=hello@yourapp.com
2. Generate a Mailable
php artisan make:mail OrderShipped --markdown=mail.orders.shipped
3. Pass data to the view
4. Send it — queue it for speed
Sending in-request makes the user wait for the mail server. Queue it so the response is instant.

use Illuminate\Support\Facades\Mail;

Mail::to($order->user)->queue(new OrderShipped($order));

faq.q: My emails are slow or time out
faq.a: You're sending inside the request. Use ->queue() instead of ->send() and run a queue worker — the request returns immediately and the worker delivers the mail.

faq.q: Emails work locally but not in production
faq.a: Check the production MAIL_* values and that the SMTP port isn't blocked by the host's firewall. Use 'log' or a tool like Mailtrap to confirm the Mailable itself is correct.

pages.h1: How to upload files to S3 (or DigitalOcean Spaces) in Laravel
pages.title: Upload Files to S3 in Laravel — Storage Guide (2026)
pages.meta_description: Upload and serve files on S3-compatible storage in Laravel: install the driver, configure the disk (AWS S3 or DigitalOcean Spaces), store uploads, and generate signed URLs.
pages.intro: Laravel's filesystem makes S3-compatible object storage a config change, not a rewrite — the same Storage API works for local disk, AWS S3, or DigitalOcean Spaces. Here's how to upload, store, and serve files.

blocks.text: 1. Install the S3 driver
composer require league/flysystem-aws-s3-v3
2. Configure the disk
Set the credentials in .env. DigitalOcean Spaces and other S3-compatible stores use the same keys plus an endpoint.

AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=...
AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=...
AWS_DEFAULT_REGION=us-east-1
AWS_BUCKET=your-bucket
# DigitalOcean Spaces / other S3-compatible:
AWS_ENDPOINT=https://nyc3.digitaloceanspaces.com
3. Store an uploaded file
4. Public files or time-limited links

faq.q: Can I use this with DigitalOcean Spaces instead of AWS?
faq.a: Yes — Spaces is S3-compatible. Use the same s3 driver, set AWS_ENDPOINT to your Spaces region URL, and everything else stays the same.

faq.q: Files upload but the URL 404s
faq.a: The object isn't public, or the URL config is off. Use storePublicly() for public files, or temporaryUrl() to generate signed links for private ones.

pages.h1: How to add full-text search in Laravel with Scout
pages.title: Add Full-Text Search in Laravel (Scout) — 2026 Guide
pages.meta_description: Add real search to Laravel with Scout: install it, make a model searchable, pick a driver (database, Meilisearch, or Algolia), index, and run typo-tolerant searches.
pages.intro: For real search — relevance ranking and typo tolerance — Laravel Scout is the clean answer. Here's how to add it, index a model, and search, plus when a simple database LIKE is enough.

blocks.text: Scout vs a plain LIKE query
For a small table and exact substrings, where('title', 'like', "%$q%") is fine. For ranked, fast, typo-tolerant search across many rows, use Scout with a search engine driver.
1. Install Scout

composer require laravel/scout
php artisan vendor:publish --provider="Laravel\Scout\ScoutServiceProvider"
2. Make a model searchable
3. Pick a driver and index
Scout supports a lightweight 'database' driver (no extra service) or engines like Meilisearch/Algolia for serious search. Set SCOUT_DRIVER in .env, then import existing rows.
php artisan scout:import "App\Models\Post"
4. Search
$results = Post::search('lravel deploy')->get(); // typo-tolerant with a real engine

faq.q: Do I need Algolia or Meilisearch to start?
faq.a: No. The 'database' driver needs no extra infrastructure and is great for small/medium tables. Move to Meilisearch (self-hosted, free) or Algolia when you need speed and typo tolerance at scale.

pages.h1: How to cache database queries in Laravel
pages.title: Cache Database Queries in Laravel (the Right Way)
pages.meta_description: Speed up Laravel by caching expensive queries with Cache::remember, choosing Redis in production, and — the part people forget — invalidating the cache when data changes.
pages.intro: Caching turns repeated, expensive queries into instant reads. Laravel's Cache facade makes it a one-liner. Here's how to cache results, pick a driver, and — the part people forget — invalidate the cache when data changes.

blocks.text: 1. Cache a query result
Cache::remember runs the query only if the key isn't cached, then stores the result for the given number of seconds.
2. Use a real cache driver in production
The default file/database drivers work but are slow. Redis is the standard for production caching.
CACHE_STORE=redis
3. Invalidate when data changes
Stale cache is the classic bug. Forget the key whenever the underlying data is updated — a model observer is a clean place to do it.
Cache::forget('posts.popular');
4. Group keys with tags (Redis)

Cache::tags('posts')->put('popular', $posts, 600);
Cache::tags('posts')->flush(); // clear the whole group

faq.q: My users see old data after an update
faq.a: You're not invalidating the cache. Call Cache::forget() (or flush a tag) whenever the cached data changes — a model observer is the cleanest place.

faq.q: Which cache driver should I use?
faq.a: Redis in production for speed and tag support. The database/file drivers are fine for local development or very low traffic.

pages.h1: How to validate a request in Laravel
pages.title: Validate a Request in Laravel — Rules & Form Requests
pages.meta_description: Validate input in Laravel two ways: a one-line $request->validate() or a reusable Form Request class. Plus how errors come back and how to customize messages.
pages.intro: Validation in Laravel can be a one-liner in the controller or a dedicated Form Request class for anything non-trivial. Here's both, plus how errors come back and how to customize messages.

blocks.text: 1. Quick inline validation
2. Form Request for reusable rules
Move complex rules into their own class for cleaner controllers and built-in authorization.
php artisan make:request StorePostRequest
3. Define rules and messages
4. Use it — validation runs automatically

faq.q: Where do validation errors go?
faq.a: On a normal form submit, Laravel redirects back with errors in the session — show them with @error in Blade. For JSON/API requests it returns a 422 response with an 'errors' object automatically.

faq.q: How do I authorize as well as validate?
faq.a: In a Form Request, return true/false from authorize(). Returning false yields a 403 before the rules run — handy for owner-only updates.

pages.h1: How to seed the database in Laravel
pages.title: Seed the Database in Laravel — Factories & Seeders
pages.meta_description: Fill your Laravel database with factories and seeders: generate realistic fake rows, call them from a seeder in the right order, and rebuild with migrate:fresh --seed.
pages.intro: Seeders fill your database with starter or test data; factories generate realistic fake rows. Together they make 'fresh database in one command' possible. Here's the full setup and the common ordering gotcha.

blocks.text: 1. Create a factory
php artisan make:factory PostFactory
2. Define realistic fake data
3. Call factories from a seeder
Seed parents before children to avoid foreign key errors — or let relationships do it for you.
4. Run it

php artisan db:seed
# or rebuild the whole schema and seed:
php artisan migrate:fresh --seed

faq.q: I get a foreign key constraint error while seeding
faq.a: You're seeding children before parents. Create the parent rows first, or use factory relationships (->has() / ->for()) so the parent is created automatically.

faq.q: Should I run seeders in production?
faq.a: Only idempotent reference data (countries, roles, plans). Never seed fake/test data into production — guard those seeders or keep them out of the production seed path.

pages.h1: How to write a feature test in Laravel
pages.title: Write a Feature Test in Laravel (2026 Guide)
pages.meta_description: Write feature tests in Laravel that hit real routes and assert on the response and database: generate a test, use RefreshDatabase, act as a logged-in user, and run the suite.
pages.intro: Feature tests hit real routes and assert on the response and the database — the highest-value tests for a web app. Here's how to write one, use a fresh database per test, and act as a logged-in user.

blocks.text: 1. Generate a test
php artisan make:test PostTest
2. Use a fresh database
The RefreshDatabase trait migrates a clean database for each test so they don't interfere with each other.
3. Test a route and the database
4. Run the suite

php artisan test
# one file:
php artisan test --filter=PostTest

faq.q: Tests are slow — how do I speed them up?
faq.a: Use an in-memory SQLite database for tests (DB_CONNECTION=sqlite, DB_DATABASE=:memory: in phpunit.xml) with RefreshDatabase, and run with --parallel to use multiple cores.

faq.q: Feature test or unit test?
faq.a: Feature tests exercise routes/HTTP and the database and catch the most real bugs — start there. Use unit tests for isolated, logic-heavy classes.

pages.h1: How to rate limit an endpoint in Laravel
pages.title: Rate Limit an Endpoint in Laravel — Throttle Guide
pages.meta_description: Protect Laravel APIs and login routes from abuse with the throttle middleware and named rate limiters: per-IP and per-user limits, plus the 429 response clients get.
pages.intro: Rate limiting protects your API and login routes from abuse. Laravel has a built-in throttle middleware plus named limiters for fine control. Here's how to apply both.

blocks.text: 1. The quick way — throttle middleware
Apply the throttle middleware with a requests-per-minute limit right on the route.
2. Named limiters for real control
Define limiters in a service provider so you can vary the limit by user, IP, or plan.
3. Apply the named limiter
4. What the client sees
When the limit is hit, Laravel returns HTTP 429 Too Many Requests with X-RateLimit-* and Retry-After headers, so clients know when to retry.

faq.q: How do I rate limit login attempts specifically?
faq.a: Use a named limiter keyed by email + IP, or Laravel's built-in login throttling (the auth scaffolding already throttles failed logins). This blunts brute-force attacks.

pages.h1: أفضل استضافة لـ Laravel في 2026 (مقارنة صادقة)
pages.title: أفضل استضافة Laravel في 2026 — مُدار مقابل VPS
pages.meta_description: مقارنة عملية بدون مبالغة لأفضل طرق استضافة تطبيق Laravel في 2026 — مُدار (Cloudways)، VPS رخيص (Hostinger)، وdroplets خام (DigitalOcean) — مع اختيار واضح لكل حالة.
pages.intro: ما كاينش استضافة 'أفضل' وحدة لـ Laravel — كاين الأفضل حسب قد أش بغيتي تخدم على السيرفر بيديك. الاختيار الحقيقي بين استضافة مُدارة (ما كتمسش السيرفر)، VPS رخيص (كتسطيه مرة وحدة)، وdroplets خام (كتحكم فكلشي). هاد المقال كيوريك كيفاش كيتقارنو الثلاثة وأشمن وحدة تختار.

blocks.text: أش كيحتاج Laravel من الاستضافة

items: PHP 8.2+ مع الإضافات المعتادة (mbstring, xml, curl, pdo)
دخول SSH باش تشغّل Composer وأوامر 'php artisan' — ماشي استضافة مشتركة عادية
قاعدة بيانات (MySQL/MariaDB أو PostgreSQL)
إمكانية تشغيل queue worker والـ scheduler (cron)
جذر الويب مُوجّه لـ public/، مع SSL مجاني

blocks.text: هاد اللائحة هي علاش الاستضافة المشتركة الرخيصة بلا SSH ما كتنفعش: ما تقدرش تشغّل artisan ولا الـ workers ولا الـ scheduler مزيان. كل الخيارات اللي تحت كتوفر هاد الشروط.
الخيار 1 — سحابة مُدارة (Cloudways): أقل خدمة على السيرفر

Cloudways كتخدم فوق مزودين بحال DigitalOcean وAWS ولكن كتدبّر السيرفر عليك: PHP مضبوط، SSL بضغطة، وstaging وGit deploy جاهزين — بلا ما تكتب أي إعداد Nginx. كتخلّص أكثر من VPS خام ولكن ما كتضيّعش وقت فإدارة السيرفر، وهادي الصفقة الصحيحة ملي وقتك أغلى من شي دولارات فالشهر.
اختارها إيلا: بغيتي التطبيق لايف اليوم، ما بغيتيش تتعلم إدارة السيرفر دابا، ولا كتخدم لكليان وكتحتاج staging ونسخ احتياطي ساهلين.
الخيار 2 — VPS رخيص (Hostinger): أقل فاتورة، كتسطيه مرة

VPS ديال Hostinger هو خيار التحكم بميزانية: كتثبّت الـ LEMP بيديك مرة، ومن بعد يولّي ديالك بثمن شهري رخيص. بعض الخطط كتجي بقالب Laravel/LEMP كيقفز ليك أغلب الإعداد. خدمة أكثر من المُدار، ولكن أرخص بكثير على المدى الطويل.
اختارها إيلا: الميزانية ضيقة، بغيتي تتعلم الجانب ديال السيرفر، وما عندكش مشكل تشغّل شوية ديال الأوامر.
الخيار 3 — Droplet خام (DigitalOcean): أكبر تحكم، رصيد للبداية

Droplet ديال DigitalOcean هو علبة Ubuntu نقية بلا ما يتفترض شي حاجة. كتاخد تحكم كامل، ورصيد الحساب الجديد (حوالي 200 دولار) كيغطّي شهور ديال droplet صغير مجاناً — مثالي للتعلم. ربطو بـ Laravel Forge من بعد إيلا بغيتي راحة المُدار بلا ما تخرج من المزود.
اختارها إيلا: بغيتي تفهم كل طبقة، كتجرّب على الرصيد المجاني، ولا ناوي تدبّر السيرفر بـ Forge.
التوصية الصادقة

items: بغيتيه لايف بلا خدمة على السيرفر ← Cloudways (مُدار).
ميزانية ضيقة وموافق تسطيه مرة ← Hostinger VPS.
كتتعلم الستاك ولا كتستعمل الرصيد المجاني ← DigitalOcean droplet.

blocks.text: إيلا ما متأكدش، بدا بالمُدار: الوقت اللي كتربح فإدارة السيرفر فالشهر الأول أغلى من فرق الثمن، وديما تقدر تنتقل لـ VPS خام ملي تعرف بالضبط أش محتاج.

faq.q: واش نقدر نستضيف Laravel على استضافة مشتركة رخيصة؟
faq.a: أحياناً، ولكن صعيب: أغلب الخطط المشتركة ما فيهاش SSH ولا Composer ولا طريقة تشغّل الـ workers والـ scheduler. استعمل سحابة مُدارة ولا VPS — بجوج رخاص ومصنوعين لتطبيقات بحال Laravel.

faq.q: واش الاستضافة المُدارة تستاهل الثمن الزائد؟
faq.a: إيلا وقتك عندو قيمة، غالباً إيه فالبداية. كتقفز إعداد السيرفر، الترقيع الأمني، وإعداد SSL. انتقل لـ VPS خام من بعد ملي تبغي فاتورة أرخص وتعرف الستاك.

faq.q: أشمن وحدة أسرع؟
faq.a: الأداء الخام متقارب فالثلاثة على عتاد متشابه — كلهم كيشغّلو نفس PHP. استعلامات قاعدة البيانات، الـ caching، وواش درتي 'config:cache' و'route:cache' كيأثرو أكثر بكثير من ماركة الاستضافة.

pages.h1: Le meilleur hébergement Laravel en 2026 (comparatif honnête)
pages.title: Meilleur hébergement Laravel 2026 — Infogéré vs VPS
pages.meta_description: Comparatif pratique et sans hype des meilleures façons d'héberger une app Laravel en 2026 — infogéré (Cloudways), VPS pas cher (Hostinger) et droplets bruts (DigitalOcean) — avec un choix clair pour chaque cas.
pages.intro: Il n'existe pas un seul « meilleur » hébergeur Laravel — il y a le meilleur selon la quantité de travail serveur que vous voulez faire. Le vrai choix se situe entre l'infogéré (vous ne touchez jamais le serveur), un VPS pas cher (configuré une fois) et les droplets bruts (vous contrôlez tout). Voici comment les trois options se comparent vraiment, et laquelle choisir.

blocks.text: Ce dont Laravel a besoin d'un hébergeur

items: PHP 8.2+ avec les extensions courantes (mbstring, xml, curl, pdo)
Un accès SSH pour lancer Composer et les commandes « php artisan » — pas du mutualisé simple
Une base de données (MySQL/MariaDB ou PostgreSQL)
La possibilité de faire tourner un worker de file et le planificateur (cron)
La racine web pointée sur public/, plus un SSL gratuit

blocks.text: Cette liste explique pourquoi le mutualisé pas cher sans SSH convient mal : vous ne pouvez pas lancer artisan, les workers ni le planificateur correctement. Toutes les options ci-dessous remplissent ces conditions.
Option 1 — Cloud infogéré (Cloudways) : le moins de travail serveur

Cloudways s'appuie sur des fournisseurs comme DigitalOcean et AWS mais gère le serveur pour vous : PHP préréglé, SSL en un clic, staging et déploiement Git intégrés — aucune config Nginx à écrire. Vous payez plus qu'un VPS brut mais ne perdez aucun temps en administration serveur, le bon compromis quand votre temps vaut plus que quelques dollars par mois.

Choisissez-le si : vous voulez l'app en ligne aujourd'hui, vous ne voulez pas encore apprendre l'administration serveur, ou vous faites du travail client et avez besoin de staging et de sauvegardes faciles.
Option 2 — VPS pas cher (Hostinger) : facture la plus basse, configuré une fois

Le VPS Hostinger est l'option contrôle-budget : vous installez la pile LEMP vous-même une fois, puis il est à vous pour un prix mensuel bas. Certaines offres incluent un modèle Laravel/LEMP qui saute l'essentiel de la configuration. Plus de travail que l'infogéré, bien moins cher sur la durée.
Choisissez-le si : votre budget est serré, vous voulez apprendre le côté serveur, et lancer quelques commandes ne vous dérange pas.
Option 3 — Droplet brut (DigitalOcean) : contrôle maximal, crédit pour démarrer

Un droplet DigitalOcean est une machine Ubuntu propre sans rien de présupposé. Vous avez le contrôle total, et le crédit de nouveau compte (environ 200 $) couvre des mois d'un petit droplet gratuitement — idéal pour apprendre. Associez-le à Laravel Forge plus tard si vous voulez le confort de l'infogéré sans quitter le fournisseur.
Choisissez-le si : vous voulez comprendre chaque couche, vous expérimentez sur le crédit gratuit, ou vous comptez gérer le serveur avec Forge.
La recommandation honnête

items: En ligne sans travail serveur → Cloudways (infogéré).
Budget serré, prêt à configurer une fois → Hostinger VPS.
Apprendre la pile ou utiliser le crédit gratuit → droplet DigitalOcean.

blocks.text: Si vous hésitez, commencez en infogéré : le temps gagné en administration serveur le premier mois vaut plus que la différence de prix, et vous pourrez toujours passer à un VPS brut une fois que vous saurez exactement ce qu'il vous faut.

faq.q: Puis-je héberger Laravel sur du mutualisé pas cher ?
faq.a: Parfois, mais c'est pénible : la plupart des offres mutualisées n'ont ni SSH, ni Composer, ni de quoi faire tourner les workers et le planificateur. Utilisez plutôt un cloud infogéré ou un VPS — les deux sont abordables et faits pour des apps comme Laravel.

faq.q: L'infogéré vaut-il le surcoût ?
faq.a: Si votre temps a de la valeur, généralement oui au début. Vous évitez la configuration serveur, les correctifs de sécurité et la config SSL. Passez à un VPS brut plus tard quand vous voulez une facture plus basse et que vous connaissez la pile.

faq.q: Lequel est le plus rapide ?
faq.a: Les performances brutes sont similaires sur du matériel comparable — tous font tourner le même PHP. Vos requêtes de base de données, le cache, et le fait d'avoir lancé « config:cache » et « route:cache » comptent bien plus que la marque de l'hébergeur.

pages.h1: كيفاش تدبلوي تطبيق Laravel على Cloudways (2026)
pages.title: نشر تطبيق Laravel على Cloudways — خطوة بخطوة (2026)
pages.meta_description: دليل واضح بالنسخ واللصق لنشر تطبيق Laravel 11 على Cloudways: إعداد السيرفر، النشر بـ Git، الـ .env، الـ migrations، الـ queue workers، وSSL.
pages.intro: Cloudways هو الطريق الأسهل لتطبيق Laravel: نسخة PHP وComposer وسيرفر الويب مضبوطين من قبل، إذن كتقفز الجزء اللي كتحارب فيه Nginx فالـ 2 ديال الليل. هاد الدليل كياخدك من سيرفر جديد لتطبيق لايف مع migrations وqueues وHTTPS — بلا ما يتفترض أي خبرة سابقة بالسيرفر.

blocks.text: 1. أطلق سيرفر وتطبيق

من بعد التسجيل، صاوب سيرفر واختار ستاك PHP. اختار منطقة قريبة من المستخدمين والخطة الأصغر فالبداية — تقدر تكبّر من بعد بلا إعادة بناء. Cloudways كيوفّر ليك خانة 'application' فين غادي يسكن كود Laravel.
ملي يطلع السيرفر، سجّل الـ IP العمومي، بيانات SSH/SFTP، والمسار العمومي ديال التطبيق (كيسالي بـ /public_html — مجلد public/ ديال Laravel كيتربط هنا).
2. صيفط الكود بـ Git
من لوحة التطبيق، حل خانة Deployment via Git، زيد مفتاح SSH ديال الريبو، واجبد الفرع main. هادي أنظف من SFTP حيت كل نشر مقبل بضغطة وحدة.

# locally, make sure your repo is clean
git add .
git commit -m "production build"
git push origin main

# then in Cloudways: Deployment via Git > Pull
3. اضبط البيئة
ادخل بـ SSH للسيرفر وسطّي الـ .env ديالك. عمرك ما تكوميتي الأسرار — ولّد مفتاح التطبيق على السيرفر.

cp .env.example .env
nano .env   # set APP_ENV=production, APP_DEBUG=false, DB_*, etc.
php artisan key:generate
composer install --optimize-autoloader --no-dev
4. Migrate وcache

php artisan migrate --force
php artisan config:cache
php artisan route:cache
php artisan view:cache
علم --force ضروري فالبرودكشن حيت Laravel كيطلب تأكيد بلاه. تخزين config وroutes فالـ cache كيعطي سرعة حقيقية فكل طلب.
5. الـ Queues والمهام المجدولة
إيلا التطبيق كيصيفط إيميلات ولا كيعالج jobs، زيد queue worker كـ Supervisor process من اللوحة، ووجّه الـ cron للـ scheduler باش أي حاجة فالـ Kernel تخدم فوقتها.
* * * * * php /path/to/artisan schedule:run >> /dev/null 2>&1
6. زيد دومين وSSL
وجّه سجل A ديال الدومين للـ IP ديال السيرفر، زيد الدومين فاللوحة، ومن بعد فعّل شهادة Let's Encrypt المجانية. حط APP_URL فالـ .env للدومين https وعاود config:cache.

faq.q: أشمن نسخة PHP نختار لـ Laravel 11؟
faq.a: Laravel 11 كيحتاج PHP 8.2 ولا أعلى. اختار 8.3 على Cloudways لأحسن توازن بين السرعة ودعم المكتبات فـ 2026.

faq.q: واش محتاج سيرفر قاعدة بيانات منفصل؟
faq.a: لا. نسخة MySQL (ولا MariaDB) كتجي فنفس السيرفر. صاوب قاعدة البيانات من اللوحة وانسخ البيانات للـ .env ديالك.

faq.q: الـ CSS/JS ما كيتحمّلش بعد النشر — علاش؟
faq.a: غالباً ما بنيتيش الأصول. شغّل 'npm install && npm run build' محلياً وكوميتي الملفات المبنية، ولا شغّل خطوة البناء على السيرفر.

pages.h1: Comment déployer une app Laravel sur Cloudways (2026)
pages.title: Déployer une app Laravel sur Cloudways — Pas à pas (2026)
pages.meta_description: Un guide clair en copier-coller pour déployer une app Laravel 11 sur Cloudways : configuration serveur, déploiement Git, .env, migrations, workers de file et SSL.
pages.intro: Cloudways est la voie la plus simple pour une app Laravel : la version de PHP, Composer et le serveur web sont déjà réglés, vous évitez donc le moment où vous vous battez avec Nginx à 2 h du matin. Ce guide vous mène d'un serveur vierge à une app en ligne avec migrations, files et HTTPS — sans aucune expérience serveur supposée.

blocks.text: 1. Lancer un serveur et une application

Après l'inscription, créez un serveur et choisissez la pile PHP. Prenez une région proche de vos utilisateurs et la plus petite offre au début — vous pourrez monter en charge plus tard sans tout refaire. Cloudways vous provisionne un emplacement « application » où vivra votre code Laravel.
Une fois le serveur prêt, notez l'IP publique, les identifiants SSH/SFTP et le chemin public de l'application (il se termine par /public_html — votre dossier public/ de Laravel y correspond).
2. Pousser le code avec Git

Depuis le tableau de bord de l'application, ouvrez le panneau Deployment via Git, ajoutez la clé SSH du dépôt et tirez la branche main. C'est plus propre que le SFTP car chaque déploiement futur se fait en un clic.

# locally, make sure your repo is clean
git add .
git commit -m "production build"
git push origin main

# then in Cloudways: Deployment via Git > Pull
3. Configurer l'environnement
Connectez-vous en SSH au serveur et préparez votre .env. Ne committez jamais les secrets — générez la clé d'application sur le serveur.

cp .env.example .env
nano .env   # set APP_ENV=production, APP_DEBUG=false, DB_*, etc.
php artisan key:generate
composer install --optimize-autoloader --no-dev
4. Migrer et mettre en cache

php artisan migrate --force
php artisan config:cache
php artisan route:cache
php artisan view:cache
L'option --force est requise en production car Laravel demande sinon une confirmation. Mettre config et routes en cache donne un vrai gain de vitesse à chaque requête.
5. Files d'attente et tâches planifiées

Si votre app envoie des mails ou traite des jobs, ajoutez un worker de file comme processus Supervisor depuis le tableau de bord, et pointez le cron vers le planificateur pour que tout ce qui est dans votre Kernel s'exécute à l'heure.
* * * * * php /path/to/artisan schedule:run >> /dev/null 2>&1
6. Ajouter un domaine et le SSL

Pointez l'enregistrement A de votre domaine vers l'IP du serveur, ajoutez le domaine dans le tableau de bord, puis activez le certificat gratuit Let's Encrypt. Mettez APP_URL dans .env sur le domaine https et relancez config:cache.

faq.q: Quelle version de PHP choisir pour Laravel 11 ?
faq.a: Laravel 11 exige PHP 8.2 ou plus. Choisissez 8.3 sur Cloudways pour le meilleur équilibre entre vitesse et support des bibliothèques en 2026.

faq.q: Ai-je besoin d'un serveur de base de données séparé ?
faq.a: Non. L'instance MySQL (ou MariaDB) est sur le même serveur. Créez la base depuis le tableau de bord et copiez les identifiants dans votre .env.

faq.q: Mon CSS/JS ne se charge pas après le déploiement — pourquoi ?
faq.a: Vous n'avez probablement pas compilé les assets. Lancez « npm install && npm run build » en local et committez les fichiers compilés, ou lancez l'étape de build sur le serveur.
